elements.sent-2411-001: This paper proposes a novel 3D speech-to-animation (STA) generation framework designed to address the shortcomings of existing models in producing diverse and emotionally resonant animations.
elements.sent-2411-002: Current STA models often generate animations that lack emotional depth and variety, failing to align with human expectations.
elements.sent-2411-003: To overcome these limitations, we introduce a novel STA model coupled with a reward model.
elements.sent-2411-004: This combination enables the decoupling of emotion and content under audio conditions through a cross-coupling training approach.
elements.sent-2411-005: Additionally, we develop a training methodology that leverages automatic quality evaluation of generated facial animations to guide the reinforcement learning process.
elements.sent-2411-006: This methodology encourages the STA model to explore a broader range of possibilities, resulting in the generation of diverse and emotionally expressive facial animations of superior quality.
elements.sent-2411-007: We conduct extensive empirical experiments on a benchmark dataset, and the results validate the effectiveness of our proposed framework in generating high-quality, emotionally rich 3D animations that are better aligned with human preferences.
elements.sent-2411-008: The growing demand for dynamic and realistic speech-driven facial animation, fueled by applications in virtual reality, gaming, and film production, has intensified research efforts in this domain \cite{fu2024mimic .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Traditional methods, often reliant on manually crafted 3D face models and scripts for controlling facial expressions, prove to be time-consuming, labor-intensive, and expensive \cite{ma20243d .}
elements.sent-2411-010: The emergence of deep learning has paved the way for end-to-end speech-driven facial animation systems, attracting significant attention in both industry and academia for their ability to generate high-quality animations with reduced production costs \cite{gururani2023space .}
elements.sent-2411-011: However, current approaches predominantly focus on achieving accurate lip synchronization \cite{wu2023speech2lip , often overlooking the subtle dynamics of facial expressions that convey emotional states.}
elements.sent-2411-012: This neglect can contribute to the 'uncanny valley' effect, where a lack of emotional realism diminishes the overall believability of the animation.
elements.sent-2411-013: Therefore, incorporating emotional expression into speech-driven 3D facial animation becomes crucial \cite{he2023speech4mesh .}
elements.sent-2411-014: While speech inherently carries emotional cues, making emotion extraction a fundamental aspect of speech comprehension \cite{hema2023emotional , separating explicit content from implicit emotional nuances in speech presents a significant challenge.}
elements.sent-2411-015: In 2D facial animation, previous methods have employed manual emotion encoding alongside content feature extraction from speech \cite{emotalk .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Although this allows for emotion modulation, manual intervention can lead to inconsistencies between the input speech and the resulting facial expressions.
elements.sent-2411-017: Such discrepancies, like pairing angry speech with happy expressions, contradict natural human communication.
elements.sent-2411-018: Furthermore, we observe three key limitations in supervised learning-based animated face generation methods: (1) Limited emotional generalization, restricting the diversity and quality of facial expressions; (2) Misalignment with human preferences, stemming from an overemphasis on imitating human facial features without considering subjective aesthetic judgments; (3) Inadequate coordination between lip movements and overall facial expressions.
elements.sent-2411-019: Drawing inspiration from the nuanced dynamics of human emotional expression, we argue that successful animation requires not only accurate lip articulation but also the conveyance of appropriate emotional cues through facial expressions.
elements.sent-2411-020: Moreover, incorporating expert feedback is essential for refining this coordination and generating harmonious and emotionally resonant animations.
elements.sent-2411-021: To address these challenges, we propose ESARM, an exploratory 3D animated face generation framework, as illustrated in Figure \ref{exm_ESARM .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Reinforcement learning (RL) is leveraged to enable an animated face agent to explore the face animation space and learn to generate emotionally expressive and harmonious animations.
elements.sent-2411-023: We formalize the audio-driven animated face generation task as a Markov decision process (MDP), where the agent receives rewards for generating animations that align with human preferences.
elements.sent-2411-024: To guide this exploration, we train a reward model (RM) based on automatically ranked animation sequences.
elements.sent-2411-025: Specifically, we first train an initial animated face generation model using paired audio and FLAME face features \cite{FLAME to provide the agent with basic animation skills.}
elements.sent-2411-026: We then generate multiple animation strategies with progressively decreasing performance by introducing noise.
elements.sent-2411-027: These noisy animations are automatically ranked and used to train the reward model.
elements.sent-2411-028: Finally, guided by the learned reward model, the animated face agent explores the animation space using RL, ultimately converging on an optimal animation strategy.
elements.sent-2411-029: The main contributions of this paper are: (1) We present a novel architecture for speech-driven, emotion-enhanced 3D facial animation, capable of generating diverse and nuanced emotional expressions.
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{minipage}{1.0\linewidth} \centerline{\includegraphics[width=16cm]{figs/arc1.png}} \end{minipage} \caption{The training process of the proposed Emotion-aware State-Action Reward Model (ESARM) and the Speech-to-Animation (STA) module.} \label{arc}
elements.sent-2411-030: Extensive research has focused on generating 2D talking heads, utilizing image- or speech-based techniques to synthesize realistic videos of individuals engaged in conversation \cite{Chen2019Hierarchical,44 .}
elements.sent-2411-031: These methods are prevalent in interactive applications such as 3D games and virtual reality environments\cite{3,39 .}
elements.sent-2411-032: A significant hurdle in the field of facial animation is the lack of diverse and high-fidelity datasets that capture the full spectrum of human emotions.
elements.sent-2411-033: However, this method primarily focuses on generating convincing mouth movements\cite{cudeiro2019capture .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Similarly, FaceFormer \cite{FaceFormer trained on the VOCASET dataset restricts the variety of facial expressions it can create.}
elements.sent-2411-035: In contrast, MeshTalk overcomes the limitations of restricted upper face motion in VOCA, which allows for the synthesis of audio-uncorrelated movements like blinking and eyebrow movements, which are crucial for conveying a wider range of emotions and adding nuance to facial expressions \cite{39 .}
elements.sent-2411-036: Existing methods offer limited semantic control over the generated 3D avatar.
elements.sent-2411-037: While both VOCA \cite{cudeiro2019capture and FaceFormer \cite{FaceFormer allow for some control over speaking style by interpolating style vectors derived from individuals in the training data, they lack a direct mechanism for manipulating emotional expressions.}}
elements.sent-2411-038: MeshTalk \cite{meshtalk , although capable of producing diverse outputs from a single audio input, does not provide explicit control over emotional content.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Karras et al. \cite{karras2017audio propose an approach that learns an emotional latent space in an unsupervised manner by jointly learning a feature vector for each training sample.}
elements.sent-2411-040: This allows for manipulation of the learned feature vector at test time.
elements.sent-2411-041: However, this learned space lacks inherent semantic meaning, necessitating manual assignment of emotions after training.
elements.sent-2411-042: Furthermore, the absence of a disentanglement mechanism implies that combining different emotion vectors with varying audio inputs may not consistently produce the desired outcome, i.e., achieving both accurate lip synchronization and the intended emotional expression.
elements.sent-2411-043: PIRL learn reward models directly from expert demonstrations, PIRL has proven effective in high-dimensional state spaces \cite{brown2020better,ibarz2018reward where AIRL may encounter challenges.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Moreover, PIRL can facilitate the integration of human feedback (RLHF) \cite{warnell2018deep , leading to improved alignment of models with human preferences.}
elements.sent-2411-045: To overcome the challenge of learning from imperfect demonstrations, T-REX \cite{brown2019extrapolating proposes a method for training a reward model.}
elements.sent-2411-046: This model is conditioned on state information and leverages ranking data provided by experts to learn a reward function.
elements.sent-2411-047: Subsequently, an agent is trained using this learned reward function to surpass the performance of the sub-optimal demonstrator.
elements.sent-2411-048: Building upon T-REX, D-REX \cite{brown2020better introduces a novel technique for automatically generating ranked demonstrations.}
elements.sent-2411-049: This is achieved by systematically injecting varying levels of noise into a policy learned through behavior cloning.
elements.sent-2411-050: This automated ranking process further enhances the ability of the reward model to learn and ultimately enables the agent to achieve superior performance.
elements.sent-2411-051: However, it is crucial to emphasize that our primary contribution is not the development of a novel PIRL algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-052: Instead, we focus on addressing the unexplored challenge of enabling emotion exploration capabilities in existing audio-driven 3D animated face generation models, an area where advancements have significant implications for the field.
elements.sent-2411-053: This study aims to synthesize sequences of 3D facial motions from a given speech signal, enabling the animation of any neutral 3D face mesh for lip-synced talking face generation.
elements.sent-2411-054: This task is inherently ill-posed, as a single speech utterance can correspond to a multitude of plausible facial animations.
elements.sent-2411-055: This ambiguity often leads cross-Model learning approaches to produce averaged motions that lack expressiveness and nuance.
elements.sent-2411-056: To address this challenge, we propose a two-step solution.
elements.sent-2411-057: First, we model the facial motion space using a learned discrete motion prior, effectively constraining the generated motions to a structured and learned representation.
elements.sent-2411-058: This prior serves to reduce the uncertainty inherent in mapping speech to facial motions.
elements.sent-2411-059: Second, we employ a speech-conditioned temporal autoregressive model to generate sequences of discrete motion tokens.
elements.sent-2411-060: This model learns the dynamic relationship between speech and facial motion, capturing the temporal evolution of facial expressions in response to the given audio.
elements.sent-2411-061: The STA model is depicted in Figure~\ref{arc (b).}
elements.sent-2411-062: The encoder leverages a pretrained model\cite{wavlm to extract a sequence of audio features, represented as $A(W)=s^{1:T}$.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Each acoustic feature, $s^t$, is subsequently enriched by the addition of a style vector, resulting in $s_s^{1:T =[S(c)^{1:T |s^{1:T ]$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: This style vector is derived by applying a linear transformation to a set of input conditioning variables, $c$, which encompass emotion, intensity, and speaker style.
elements.sent-2411-065: The styling function, $S(c)$, performs this linear projection.
elements.sent-2411-066: In the training phase, the conditioning variables, $c$, are constructed using the corresponding ground truth values for emotion type, emotion intensity, and speaker identity.
elements.sent-2411-067: During inference, $c$ can be manually adjusted, providing animators with control over the emotional expression of the generated facial animation.
elements.sent-2411-068: After applying the style vector, the resulting speech representation is then mapped onto the latent space of the motion prior model.
elements.sent-2411-069: Drawing inspiration from the work of Ji et al. \cite{19 , the emotion disentangling encoder specifically designed for the task of 3D facial animation generation.}
elements.sent-2411-070: It is the first instance where the concept of emotion disentanglement has been explicitly applied within this domain.
elements.sent-2411-071: Our proposed module offers a refined and streamlined approach compared to the original disentanglement framework, introducing several key improvements and simplifications.
elements.sent-2411-072: Initially, we employ the pre-trained model~\cite{wavlm to extract high-level acoustic features from the input speech instead of MFCC.}
elements.sent-2411-073: This model has been shown to be highly effective in capturing the nuances of speech audio.
elements.sent-2411-074: Secondly, we streamline the disentanglement process for improved clarity and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-075: Finally, we re-architect the module as an end-to-end system, enabling it to directly generate the 52 blendshape coefficients that are fundamental for driving realistic facial animation.
elements.sent-2411-076: This streamlined design eliminates the need for any intermediate processing steps, allowing for a more efficient and integrated workflow.
elements.sent-2411-077: By directly predicting these essential animation parameters, our system simplifies the process of generating expressive facial movements from speech input.
elements.sent-2411-078: This end-to-end approach provides stronger constraints during training, leading to more accurate and expressive facial animations.
elements.sent-2411-079: To provide high-quality data for reward model training and establish a baseline proficiency for exploration, an initial policy, denoted as $G_{STA}$, is trained on a human expert dataset, $\mathcal{D _{human}$, using supervised learning.}
elements.sent-2411-080: This process follows the methodology outlined in \cite{emote , including the network architecture, cross-entropy loss, and discretization of the motion space.}
elements.sent-2411-081: This stage focuses on utilizing the pre-trained STA model ($G_{STA}$) to acquire demonstrations with automatic ranking.
elements.sent-2411-082: Policies with performance levels ranging from the behavior cloning policy ($G_{STA}$) to a completely randomized policy are obtained by introducing varying degrees of noise into the pre-trained model, similar to the approach employed in D-REX \cite{brown2020better .}
elements.sent-2411-083: Empirical observations indicate an inverse correlation between the level of noise introduced and the agent's performance.
elements.sent-2411-084: A higher degree of noise results in lower performance, while a lower degree of noise leads to better performance.
elements.sent-2411-085: This observation allows us to automatically rank the generated demonstrations based on the noise level used during their generation.
elements.sent-2411-086: We adopt an $\epsilon$-greedy strategy for injecting noise during the demonstration collection phase.
elements.sent-2411-087: This strategy involves a probabilistic approach to action selection at each decision point.
elements.sent-2411-088: With a probability of $1-\epsilon$, the agent exploits its current knowledge and chooses the action deemed most promising according to its learned policy.
elements.sent-2411-089: However, with a probability of $\epsilon$, the agent deviates from its learned policy and instead explores the action space by randomly sampling an action.
elements.sent-2411-090: This exploration component allows the agent to discover potentially better actions and refine its policy over time.
elements.sent-2411-091: This process generates a dataset of ranked trajectories, where trajectories generated with higher noise levels are ranked lower than those generated with lower noise levels.
elements.sent-2411-092: The reward model is trained to assess the quality of generated facial animations by learning from a dataset of ranked demonstrations.
elements.sent-2411-093: This learning process allows the model to internalize the nuances of high-quality facial expressions and movements.
elements.sent-2411-094: We postulate that a discriminative model, such as our reward model, possesses a superior capacity for extrapolation in comparison to autoregressive models.
elements.sent-2411-095: This advantage stems from the discriminative model's ability to directly learn the underlying mapping between input features (e.g., speech audio) and output labels (e.g., animation quality), rather than relying on sequential prediction as in autoregressive models.
elements.sent-2411-096: This direct mapping facilitates better generalization to unseen data and scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-097: The reward model takes as input an animated talking face sequence, which includes both audio and facial motion information.
elements.sent-2411-098: To maintain causality, the two Modelities are interleaved and then processed by a causal transformer.
elements.sent-2411-099: This transformer employs masked multi-head attention to capture temporal dependencies within the sequence.
elements.sent-2411-100: The output of the transformer is then used to predict a reward.
elements.sent-2411-101: The reward model is trained using a pairwise ranking loss.
elements.sent-2411-102: Pairs of trajectories with different quality levels, ${\tau_i,\tau_j}$, are sampled from the ranked dataset, and the model is trained to predict the relative ranking of each pair based on their total rewards.
elements.sent-2411-103: This training process effectively teaches the reward model to distinguish between high-quality and low-quality facial animations.
elements.sent-2411-104: This is achieved by minimizing the cross-entropy loss: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}_{RM}=-\mathbb{E}_{(\tau_i,\tau_j,y)\in\mathcal{D}} [(1-y)\log\mathrm{R}(\tau_i\prec\tau_j;\theta) +y\log\mathrm{R}(\tau_j\prec\tau_i;\theta)], \label{eq:rmloss} \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $y=\text{int (\epsilon_i<\epsilon_j)$ and $\mathrm{R (\tau_i\prec\tau_j;\theta)$ is the ranking predictor.}}
elements.sent-2411-105: The final stage involves utilizing the trained reward model, $R_M$, to guide the learning of a reinforcement learning policy, $\pi_{RL}$ or $\pi_\phi$.
elements.sent-2411-106: The reward model provides a sparse reward signal, which is only given at the end of each generated sequence.
elements.sent-2411-107: This sparse reward encourages the policy to learn long-term dependencies and generate high-quality facial animations that are consistent with the entire speech utterance.
elements.sent-2411-108: The policy network is trained to maximize the expected return, which is the cumulative discounted reward obtained over the entire sequence: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \phi = \arg\max_{\phi} \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_{\phi}} [R(\tau)] = \arg\max_{\phi} \sum_{\tau} p_{\tau \sim \pi_{\phi}} R(\tau) \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $p_{\tau\sim\pi_\phi (\tau)$ is the probability of generating motion $\tau$ under the policy $\pi_\phi$, and $R(\tau)=\sum_{t=0 ^{T-1}\gamma^tR(s_t,a_t)$ is the discounted return of the motion $\tau$.}}
elements.sent-2411-109: In the context of audio-conditioned facial animation generation, we simplify the policy optimization process and directly apply an autoregressive model, such as a transformer, for facial motion generation.
elements.sent-2411-110: This study utilizes the MEAD dataset \cite{MEAD for training the ESARM model.}
elements.sent-2411-111: MEAD is a comprehensive emotional video dataset featuring 48 individuals reciting approximately 30 short English sentences with varying emotional expressions.
elements.sent-2411-112: Each participant delivers the sentences with a neutral expression and three different intensities of seven basic emotions.
elements.sent-2411-113: Due to the limited availability of public 3D talking face datasets with emotional expressions, this study leverages facial blend shapes as supervisory signals to reconstruct realistic 3D faces from the 2D MEAD dataset.
elements.sent-2411-114: A refined blend shape capture technique extracts blend shape coefficients from the dataset, effectively capturing subtle nuances of human emotional expressions.
elements.sent-2411-115: To evaluate the accuracy of lip synchronization, we used the lip vertex error (LVE) metric, consistent with the evaluation methodology in MeshTalk \cite{meshtalk and FaceFormer \cite{FaceFormer .}}
elements.sent-2411-116: Recognizing that LVE alone does not fully capture the subtleties of emotional expression, we introduced an additional metric called emotional vertex error (EVE).
elements.sent-2411-117: The calculation of EVE begins with the identification of specific vertex indices within the FLAME template that correspond to the eye and forehead regions.
elements.sent-2411-118: These vertices serve as landmarks for measuring the facial expressions associated with eyebrow movements, which are crucial for conveying emotions like surprise and anger.
elements.sent-2411-119: By precisely pinpointing these key vertices, we can accurately quantify the subtle changes in eyebrow shape and position that contribute to the overall expressiveness of the facial animation.
elements.sent-2411-120: Lip Synchronization: A comparative analysis was performed between our model, FaceFormer~\cite{FaceFormer , and EmoTalk~\cite{emotalk using identical audio inputs to generate corresponding facial animations.}}
elements.sent-2411-121: The results show in Figure \ref{f4 indicate that our proposed model exhibits more pronounced lip movements and a higher degree of alignment with human speech patterns.}
elements.sent-2411-122: Moreover, the model demonstrates mouth closure capabilities comparable to models trained on high-precision 3D scanned facial datasets.
elements.sent-2411-123: Emotional Expression: As illustrated in Figure \ref{f4 , our method effectively captures and conveys subtle emotional variations in facial expressions, demonstrating a clear advantage over previous approaches.}
elements.sent-2411-124: These earlier methods, not specifically designed with emotional expressiveness as a primary objective, often exhibited limited facial expressiveness and struggled to accurately reflect the emotional nuances present in different speech patterns.
elements.sent-2411-125: In contrast, our approach demonstrates a heightened sensitivity to emotional cues, resulting in more natural and expressive facial animations that are well-aligned with the emotional content of the speech.
elements.sent-2411-126: It shows the ability to effectively leverage both acoustic and linguistic information to generate nuanced and emotionally rich facial expressions.
elements.sent-2411-127: For instance, in expressing anger, distinct eyebrow raising and lowering movements accurately reflect the emotional information conveyed through speech.
elements.figure-2411-005: \begin{minipage}{1.0\linewidth} \centerline{\includegraphics[width=7.5cm]{figs/exp.png}} \end{minipage} \caption{Qualitative comparisons of facial movements generated by different methods on the MEAD dataset reveal notable distinctions.} \label{f4}
elements.figure-2411-006: \caption{Lip Vertex Error (LVE) and Emotional Vertex Error (EVE) on RAVDESS and MEAD datasets.} \begin{tabular}{lcccc} Method \multicolumn{2}{c}{RAVDESS} \multicolumn{2}{c}{MEAD} \cmidrule(lr){2-3} \cmidrule(lr){4-5} LVE EVE LVE EVE MeshTalk~\cite{meshtalk} 4.081 4.296 4.328 3.697 FaceFormer~\cite{FaceFormer} 3.439 3.657 3.951 3.256 EmoTalk~\cite{emotalk} 3.468 3.842 3.367 3.065 Ours 2.891 2.587 2.982 2.485 \end{tabular} \label{t1}
elements.sent-2411-128: As shown in Table \ref{t1 , the facial animation sequences generated by ESARM achieve optimal values for both LVE and EVE, demonstrating the effectiveness and accuracy of ESARM in generating facial expressions with emotions.}
elements.sent-2411-129: Furthermore, to highlight the alignment with human preferences achieved through exploration, Figure \ref{f4 presents a comparison.}
elements.sent-2411-130: The upper animation sequence was generated by the STA agent, while the lower sequence was generated by the RL fine-tuned agent.
elements.sent-2411-131: Through exploration, the fine-tuned STA agent can generate complex and emotionally expressive actions that are consistent with human preferences.
elements.sent-2411-132: These actions are present in the human dataset, but only the RL agent, and not the STA agent, was able to reproduce them.
elements.sent-2411-133: Notably, the agent does not have access to any human datasets during the reinforcement learning fine-tuning process.
elements.sent-2411-134: We hypothesize that this phenomenon arises because the objective of STA (predicting the next action code for animated face sequences in the training set) differs from the human preference for animated sequences.
elements.sent-2411-135: The frequency of different actions during inference matches their frequency in the training set, rather than aligning with human preferences, resulting in the generation of common actions during inference.
elements.sent-2411-136: Subsequently, during reinforcement learning, the policy is optimized to generate actions that are more aligned with human expression preferences, guided by the reward model.

elements.subsection-2411-008: Impact of Noise Level on Demonstrations
elements.sent-2411-137: The reward model we trained is based on the idea that a policy learning to copy human behavior will be much better than a policy that acts randomly, and that adding noise will make this policy worse.
elements.sent-2411-138: To test this, we added different amounts of noise [0.02, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 1.00] to the training music data and measured how well the imitation learning policies performed.
elements.sent-2411-139: As illustrated in Table \ref{t2 , a general trend of decreasing animation quality is observed with increasing noise levels.}
elements.sent-2411-140: This paper introduces a novel approach to creating emotive 3D facial animations driven by speech.
elements.sent-2411-141: Our method includes the Speech-to-Animation (STA) model and an optimization phase based on a reward model.
elements.sent-2411-142: The STA model disentangles speech into emotional and content segments, thereby improving the emotional clarity of facial animations.
elements.sent-2411-143: By leveraging speaker information, it generates initial facial actions for speech animation.
elements.sent-2411-144: The optimization phase enhances the STA model by introducing noise to create a reward model, improving facial expressiveness based on human preferences.
elements.sent-2411-145: Experimental results demonstrate superior performance compared to existing methods.
elements.sent-2411-146: This work advances virtual reality applications by offering more authentic and engaging emotional interactions through lifelike facial expressions.
